title: Proposal
text: GLP-1 receptor signaling may regulate downstream genes involved in the cAMP/PKA pathway. In this project, we analyze publicly available data to evaluate whether tissues with higher GLP-1R expression show greater downstream pathway gene activity and whether treatment is associated with increased expression of GLP-1R and its downstream signalling genes compared with untreated samples.

title: Proposal
text: GLP-1 receptor signaling may regulate downstream genes involved in the cAMP/PKA pathway. In this project, we analyze publicly available data to evaluate whether tissues with higher GLP-1R expression show greater downstream pathway gene activity and whether treatment is associated with increased expression of GLP-1R and its downstream signalling genes compared with untreated samples.

title: Proposal
text:
Dataset(s)

Source: NCBI GEO Public Dataset (GSE293291)
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/query/acc.cgi?acc=GSE293291
Sample size: 60 samples
Key variables:

Tissue type
Genotype
Treatment type
Gene expression measurements

License/usage: The dataset is publicly available through GEO and includes downloadable processed expression files as well as raw data in SRA. The GEO accession page does not list a separate explicit reuse license, so we cite the GEO accession page when using the data.

title: Proposal
text:
Planned analysis
Describe your plan in bullets:

Data cleaning and preprocessing

Combine expression files into one dataset
Match metadata to expression matrix (GSM IDs)
Log2 transformation and filtering of low-expression genes
Remove zero-variance genes before analysis

Cleaning/QC

Perform PCA to identify clustering and outliers
Evaluate variance explained by principal components
Check separation by treatment and genotype

Visualizations

PCA plots for QC
Volcano plots for differential expression
Heatmaps of top differentially expressed genes

Statistical/ML approach

Build design matrix based on genotype, treatment, and tissues
Fit linear model using limma
Identify differentially expressed genes

Validation & Intrepretation

Compare treated vs untreated groups

Peptide Treatment: mGIPR-Ab/P1

Vehicle Treatment

Focus on balanced comaprisons
Interpret results in context of GLP-1R and cAMP/PKA pathway.

title: Proposal
text:

Any optional stretch goals

title: GLP-1R Expression and cAMP/PKA Signaling
text:
BNFO 420 Capstone — VCU
Investigating how GLP-1 receptor activation affects downstream gene expression across liver and fat tissue in obese mice using bulk RNA-seq analysis.

title: GLP-1R Expression and cAMP/PKA Signaling
text:
Abstract

GLP-1 receptor agonists have become a major class of therapeutics for obesity and type 2 diabetes, yet the transcriptional mechanisms through which they exert their effects across peripheral tissues remain incompletely understood. This project investigates whether GLP-1R activation is associated with upregulation of downstream cAMP/PKA pathway genes in liver and inguinal white adipose tissue (iWAT) using publicly available bulk RNA-seq data from a mouse model.
We analyzed 60 samples spanning four genotypes, two treatment conditions, and two tissue types using a pipeline that included log2 normalization, low-expression gene filtering, PCA-based quality control, and differential expression analysis with Limma. Our results showed that drug treatment produced modest transcriptional changes in liver but a stronger response in iWAT, with cAMP/PKA pathway genes trending in the predicted direction without reaching full statistical significance. Unexpectedly, CNS-specific deletion of the GIP receptor produced the largest number of differentially expressed genes across all comparisons, suggesting a previously underappreciated role for GIPR in regulating peripheral tissue gene expression through the brain-liver axis. These findings partially support our original hypothesis while revealing a more complex and tissue-specific regulatory landscape than initially predicted.

title: GLP-1R Expression and cAMP/PKA Signaling
text:
Key Findings

Drug effect was real but subtle — treated and vehicle samples separated clearly in PCA, but cAMP/PKA pathway genes did not reach strong significance.


Fat tissue responded more strongly than liver — iWAT showed more differentially expressed genes with treatment than liver did.


GIPR knockout had the largest effect — unexpectedly, deleting GIPR from CNS neurons caused more gene expression changes than either drug treatment or GLP-1R deletion, pointing to an important brain-liver axis through GIPR.

title: GLP-1R Expression and cAMP/PKA Signaling
text:
Project Roadmap


1


Download and Organize Data
Downloaded 4 expression files from GEO (GSE293291), combined 60 samples into one matrix, and built a metadata table mapping each sample to its genotype, treatment, and tissue.




2


Normalize and Filter
Log2 transformed FPKM values and filtered 54,343 genes down to 14,152 in liver and 14,715 in iWAT by removing low-expression genes that would add noise.




3


Quality Control with PCA
Ran PCA to confirm samples clustered correctly by treatment group before running any statistics. Both tissues showed clear separation between treated and vehicle samples.




4


Differential Expression with Limma
Used Limma to test four comparisons — treatment effect in liver, treatment effect in iWAT, GLP1R knockout vs control, and GIPR knockout vs control.




5


Visualize and Interpret
Generated volcano plots for all four comparisons, a heatmap of the top 10 DEGs, and a gene filtering bar chart. Interpreted results in the context of our original hypothesis.

title: Team
text:
Analysis Pipeline Overview
Our team built a complete RNA-seq analysis pipeline from scratch:

Data Acquisition (Mariya) — Downloaded and organized 60 samples from GEO accession GSE293291
Metadata Organization (Khadeeja) — Created sample annotation table linking files to experimental conditions
Normalization & Filtering (Zoya) — Quality control and gene filtering (54,343 → 14,152 genes)
Exploratory Analysis (Mariya) — PCA plots to validate data quality and sample clustering
Differential Expression (Khadeeja) — Limma analysis across four experimental comparisons
Visualization (Courtney & Mariya) — Volcano plots and heatmaps of key findings
Interpretation & Communication (Courtney & Saja) — Scientific synthesis and presentation design
Website Development (Zoya) — Project documentation and results dissemination

title: Results
text: This page presents all figures generated from our differential expression analysis pipeline. Each figure is organized by the question it answers, with a short interpretation underneath.

title: Results
text:
Quality Control

Gene Filtering Summary
Before running any statistics we filtered 54,343 genes down to the ones that were actively expressed across our samples.

Liver: 14,152 genes retained
iWAT: 14,715 genes retained
Genes removed had expression near zero across all samples and would have added noise to the analysis




Gene Filtering Bar Chart





PCA — Liver GLP1R Cohort
PCA confirmed that treated and vehicle samples separate into distinct clusters in liver tissue. PC1 captured 26.4% of variance and PC2 captured 15.3%, with treatment driving the primary axis of variation. This validated our dataset before running differential expression.



PCA Liver GLP1R





PCA — iWAT GLP1R Cohort
Fat tissue showed even clearer separation between treated and vehicle samples than liver did. PC1 captured 33.6% of variance, suggesting treatment has a stronger global effect on gene expression in iWAT than in liver.



PCA iWAT GLP1R

title: Results
text:
Differential Expression

Volcano Plot 1 — Liver Treatment Effect
Question: Does the drug change gene expression in liver?
Result: 8 genes upregulated, 49 downregulated. Modest response. cAMP/PKA hypothesis genes did not reach significance, clustering near zero fold change. The drug is having a real but subtle effect in liver.



Volcano Plot Liver Treatment





Volcano Plot 2 — GLP1R Knockout vs Control in Liver
Question: What happens when GLP-1R is removed from CNS neurons?
Result: 87 genes upregulated, 173 downregulated. Far more changes than drug treatment alone. Prkar2a and Prkacb trending close to significance (p = 0.024 and 0.041), suggesting the cAMP/PKA pathway responds when GLP-1R signaling is eliminated.



Volcano Plot GLP1R KO





Volcano Plot 3 — GIPR Knockout vs Control in Liver
Question: What happens when GIPR is removed from CNS neurons?
Result: 299 genes upregulated, 161 downregulated. The largest response across all four comparisons. This was our most unexpected finding — GIPR deletion affected liver gene expression more broadly than GLP-1R deletion, pointing to an important brain-liver axis through GIPR.



Volcano Plot GIPR KO





Volcano Plot 4 — iWAT Treatment Effect
Question: Does the drug affect fat tissue differently than liver?
Result: 49 genes upregulated, 192 downregulated. Stronger than liver. Prkar2a and Prkacb trending at p = 0.034 and 0.042 — the same cAMP/PKA genes that appeared in the GLP1R knockout comparison, suggesting a consistent but subtle biological signal.



Volcano Plot iWAT Treatment

title: Results
text:
Heatmap

Top 10 Differentially Expressed Genes
The heatmap shows the top 10 most significantly changed genes from the liver treatment comparison. Treated and vehicle samples cluster on opposite sides, visually confirming the drug produces a consistent and reproducible pattern of gene expression changes across all samples.



Heatmap Top 10 DEGs





Top 3 Most Significant Genes
This heatmap focuses on the three most significantly differentially expressed genes from the liver treatment comparison. The pattern of expression across treated and vehicle samples is even clearer here, confirming that these genes are the strongest and most consistent responders to the drug treatment.



Heatmap Top 3 DEGs

title: Results
text:
Synthesis
Across all figures, the key findings are:

The drug is having a real biological effect confirmed by PCA separation and heatmap clustering
cAMP/PKA pathway genes trend in the predicted direction but do not cross our significance threshold
Fat tissue responds more strongly to treatment than liver
GIPR deletion in the CNS produces the largest transcriptional changes of any comparison, pointing to a brain-liver axis through GIPR that was not part of our original hypothesis

Our hypothesis is partially supported. The direction of change is consistent with our predictions but the magnitude is more subtle than expected, and GIPR emerged as an equally important regulatory system alongside GLP-1R.

title: Introduction
text: GLP-1 receptor agonists like semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy) and tirzepatide (Mounjaro) have revolutionized treatment for type 2 diabetes and obesity. However, the molecular mechanisms underlying their therapeutic effects remain incompletely understood. This project investigates how GLP-1 receptor (GLP-1R) signaling affects gene expression in metabolically active tissues using RNA-seq data from mice treated with a dual GLP-1R/GIPR agonist (mGIPR-Ab/P1). Our analysis revealed tissue-specific responses, unexpected GIPR dominance in regulating peripheral gene expression, and modest transcriptional changes in the predicted cAMP/PKA pathway.

title: Introduction
text: GLP-1 receptor agonists like semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy) and tirzepatide (Mounjaro) have revolutionized treatment for type 2 diabetes and obesity. However, the molecular mechanisms underlying their therapeutic effects remain incompletely understood. This project investigates how GLP-1 receptor (GLP-1R) signaling affects gene expression in metabolically active tissues using RNA-seq data from mice treated with a dual GLP-1R/GIPR agonist (mGIPR-Ab/P1). Our analysis revealed tissue-specific responses, unexpected GIPR dominance in regulating peripheral gene expression, and modest transcriptional changes in the predicted cAMP/PKA pathway.

title: Introduction
text:
Background Knowledge on GLP-1
GLP-1 (glucagon-like peptide-1) is an incretin hormone secreted by intestinal L-cells in response to food intake. It plays a central role in glucose homeostasis and energy regulation by:

Enhancing insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells in a glucose-dependent manner
Suppressing glucagon release from alpha cells
Slowing gastric emptying to reduce post-meal glucose spikes
Promoting satiety through central nervous system pathways

The GLP-1 receptor is a G-protein coupled receptor (GPCR) expressed in multiple tissues including pancreas, brain, heart, kidney, liver, and adipose tissue. Upon ligand binding, GLP-1R activates the cAMP/PKA signaling pathway, which drives downstream transcriptional changes. This pathway includes key genes such as PRKACA, PRKACB, and CREB1 that regulate metabolic gene expression.

title: Introduction
text:
Gap in Knowledge
While GLP-1 drugs are clinically effective, several questions remain unanswered:

Which genes are directly regulated by GLP-1R signaling in peripheral metabolic tissues like liver and fat?
Does GLP-1R activation robustly upregulate the cAMP/PKA pathway genes at the transcriptional level, or does it work through post-translational mechanisms?
How does tissue-specific gene expression differ between liver and adipose tissue in response to GLP-1 treatment?
What is the relative contribution of GIPR (glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptor), which is often co-targeted with GLP-1R in dual agonist drugs?
Do receptor knockout models reveal compensatory mechanisms or confirm direct receptor-mediated transcriptional effects?

Understanding these mechanisms could inform the development of more targeted therapies and explain why dual GLP-1R/GIPR agonists show enhanced efficacy compared to single-receptor drugs.

title: Introduction
text:
What is GLP-1R?
GLP-1R (Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor) is a class B GPCR that mediates the physiological effects of GLP-1 hormone. Key characteristics include:

Tissue distribution: Pancreatic islets, brain (hypothalamus, brainstem), heart, liver, adipose tissue, kidney
Signaling mechanism: Primarily through Gαs protein → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA activation → CREB phosphorylation → gene transcription
Therapeutic targeting: GLP-1R agonists (exenatide, liraglutide, semaglutide) are FDA-approved for diabetes and weight management
CNS-mediated effects: Central GLP-1R activation contributes to appetite suppression and energy expenditure regulation

GIPR (Glucose-Dependent Insulinotropic Polypeptide Receptor) is a related incretin receptor that responds to GIP hormone. It shares similar downstream signaling pathways and tissue distribution with GLP-1R. Dual GLP-1R/GIPR agonists like tirzepatide show superior efficacy compared to GLP-1R-only drugs, suggesting complementary or synergistic mechanisms.
The mGIPR-Ab/P1 agonist used in this study is a dual agonist that activates both GLP-1R and GIPR, allowing us to assess combined incretin signaling effects.

Dependent Variable: Expression levels of cAMP/PKA pathway genes and genome-wide gene expression patterns

title: Introduction
text:
Relevant Literature

Key Studies on Incretin Biology
Campbell, J. E., & Drucker, D. J. (2013). Pharmacology, physiology, and mechanisms of incretin hormone action. Cell Metabolism, 17(6), 819-837.
- Comprehensive review of GLP-1 and GIP signaling mechanisms, tissue distribution, and therapeutic applications
Müller, T. D., et al. (2019). Glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1). Molecular Metabolism, 30, 72-130.
- Detailed overview of GLP-1 biology, including CNS-mediated metabolic effects and receptor signaling pathways
Holst, J. J., & Rosenkilde, M. M. (2020). GIP as a therapeutic target in diabetes and obesity: Insight from incretin co-agonists. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 105(8), e2710-e2716.
- Discusses the rationale for dual GLP-1R/GIPR agonists and the complementary roles of these receptors


Methodological References
Ritchie, M. E., et al. (2015). limma powers differential expression analyses for RNA-sequencing and microarray studies. Nucleic Acids Research, 43(7), e47.
- The statistical method (Limma) used for differential expression analysis in this project
Love, M. I., Huber, W., & Anders, S. (2014). Moderated estimation of fold change and dispersion for RNA-seq data with DESeq2. Genome Biology, 15(12), 550.
- Reference for normalization methods used in RNA-seq analysis


Dataset Source
Zhu, Z., et al. (2024). GSE293291: RNA-seq analysis of liver and adipose tissue from GLP-1R and GIPR knockout mice treated with mGIPR-Ab/P1. NCBI Gene Expression Omnibus.
- The published dataset used in this project, containing approximately 54,000 genes measured across 60 samples representing: - Genotypes: Wild-type (Glp1r fl/fl), CNS-specific GLP-1R knockout (Glp1r Wnt1-/-), CNS-specific GIPR knockout (Gipr Syn-/-) - Treatments: Vehicle control, mGIPR-Ab/P1 dual agonist - Tissues: Liver and inguinal white adipose tissue (iWAT)

title: Introduction
section: The GIP Receptor and Its Unexpected Role
text:
The GIP Receptor and Its Unexpected Role
While our project began with a focus on GLP-1R signaling, the glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptor (GIPR) emerged as a critically important and unexpected finding throughout our analysis.

What is GIPR?
The GIP receptor is a G protein-coupled receptor that binds glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP), the other major incretin hormone alongside GLP-1. Like GLP-1R, GIPR is expressed in multiple tissues including the pancreas, brain, adipose tissue, and liver. Upon activation, GIPR also signals through the cAMP/PKA pathway, stimulating insulin secretion and influencing energy metabolism.
Historically GIP was considered less therapeutically relevant than GLP-1 because early studies suggested that GIP-stimulated insulin secretion was impaired in people with type 2 diabetes. However more recent research has revealed that GIPR plays important roles in fat metabolism, bone density, and energy balance that go beyond its incretin function.


GIPR in the Brain
Both GLP-1R and GIPR are expressed in the central nervous system, particularly in regions involved in appetite regulation and energy homeostasis such as the hypothalamus and brainstem. CNS-specific receptor signaling has been shown to influence peripheral metabolic outcomes through neural circuits that communicate with peripheral organs including the liver and adipose tissue.
Our project used a CNS-specific GIPR knockout model (Gipr Syn-/-) where the GIP receptor was deleted specifically from synapsin-expressing neurons in the brain. This allowed us to isolate the contribution of brain GIPR signaling to peripheral gene expression without affecting GIPR in other tissues.


What Our Data Showed
Our GIPR knockout comparison produced the most striking result of the entire project. When we compared Gipr Syn-/- mice against Gipr fl/fl controls in liver tissue, we found 299 upregulated and 161 downregulated genes — far more than any other comparison including the drug treatment itself and the GLP-1R knockout.
This was unexpected for two reasons. First, our original hypothesis focused primarily on GLP-1R as the main driver of downstream signaling. Second, the magnitude of the GIPR effect in liver was larger than the GLP-1R effect, suggesting that CNS GIPR signaling may play a broader regulatory role in peripheral liver gene expression than previously appreciated.


What This Means
This finding points to what may be an important brain-liver axis mediated through GIPR. The brain appears to use the GIP receptor to regulate gene expression in peripheral tissues like the liver, and this regulation is substantial enough to produce more transcriptional changes than pharmacological activation of GLP-1R.
This does not contradict the established role of GLP-1R in metabolic regulation. Rather it suggests that the two receptor systems work in parallel and may have complementary roles — GLP-1R signaling may be more important for acute metabolic responses while GIPR in the CNS may contribute to baseline transcriptional regulation of peripheral tissues.


Implications for Dual Agonist Therapy
The drug used in our study, mGIPR-Ab/P1, is a dual-acting compound that blocks GIPR while activating GLP-1R. Our finding that GIPR deletion causes large transcriptional changes in liver raises an interesting question about how this dual mechanism contributes to the drug’s overall therapeutic effect. Future research should examine whether the transcriptional changes we observed in the GIPR knockout overlap with changes caused by the drug, which could help clarify the specific contribution of GIPR blockade to the drug’s metabolic benefits.

title: Introduction
text:
Objective
This project aims to characterize the transcriptional response to GLP-1R signaling in liver and adipose tissue using published RNA-seq data from GEO (GSE293291). The dataset includes 60 samples across 4 genotypes, 2 treatment conditions, and 2 tissues.
Specific objectives include:

Test the hypothesis that GLP-1R activation increases expression of cAMP/PKA pathway genes (PRKACA, PRKACB, CREB1, etc.)
Compare treatment effects (mGIPR-Ab/P1 dual agonist drug) with receptor knockout models (CNS-specific GLP-1R knockout and GIPR knockout) to distinguish direct receptor-mediated effects
Evaluate tissue-specific responses between liver and inguinal white adipose tissue (iWAT)
Assess the relative importance of GIPR versus GLP-1R in regulating peripheral gene expression through CNS-mediated pathways
Identify differentially expressed genes that may contribute to the metabolic benefits of incretin-based therapies

title: Introduction
text:
Research Question and Hypothesis
Research Question:
Do cells that express GLP-1R show increased expression of downstream cAMP/PKA pathway genes in response to receptor activation?
Hypothesis:
We predicted that treatment with the mGIPR-Ab/P1 dual agonist would strongly upregulate GLP-1R expression and activate downstream cAMP/PKA pathway genes in both liver and adipose tissue, with similar effects across both tissues.
Independent Variable: Treatment condition (vehicle control vs. mGIPR-Ab/P1 dual agonist)
